title: Numbered Registers
subtitle: "0 = last yank. "1–"9 = the last nine deletes.
summary: "0 holds your most recent yank; it never updates on delete. "1 holds your most recent multi-character delete; "2 the one before, and so on through "9. They shift on every delete.

blocks.text: Vim quietly keeps a backlog of your recent yanks and deletes. {key:"}{key:0} is your most recent yank. {key:"}{key:1} through {key:"}{key:9} are the last nine deletes — they shift down on each new one.

blocks.title: The Two Different Update Rules
blocks.text:
**{key:"}{key:0}** — only updated on **yank**. Stays untouched by delete and change. So if you yank, then delete, then paste, you get the *delete*. To get your yank back: {key:"}{key:0}{key:p}.

**{key:"}{key:1}–{key:"}{key:9}** — a sliding window of the last nine **deletes** (and changes), not yanks. On each new delete:

  • Old {key:"}{key:9} falls off.
  • {key:"}{key:8} → {key:"}{key:9}.
  • {key:"}{key:7} → {key:"}{key:8}. … {key:"}{key:1} → {key:"}{key:2}. New delete → {key:"}{key:1}.

Exception: small charwise deletes (less than a line, no newline) go into {key:"}{key:-}, the small-delete register, and *don't* shift the numbered registers. So {key:x} doesn't bump anything off — only multi-character or line deletes do.

With both rules in mind, the entire register layout becomes navigable: you can paste the most recent yank, the most recent delete, or the delete before that — all by name.

blocks.title: The .  Trick (Numbered Register + Dot)
blocks.text:
Combine numbered registers with the dot command and you get a power move:

  1. Delete a line with {key:d}{key:d}. It goes to {key:"}{key:1}.
  2. Move somewhere else.
  3. {key:"}{key:1}{key:p} pastes the deleted line.
  4. {key:.} repeats — but here's the surprise: {key:.} *increments* the numbered-register reference. So the next {key:.} pastes from {key:"}{key:2}, then {key:"}{key:3}, etc.

This lets you walk back through your delete history one paste at a time. See [`recipes.numbered-paste`](#recipes.numbered-paste).
